data.title: Baron Asset Fund adds Axon in Q4, sells Trade Desk
data.description:
• Baron Asset Fund gained 7.89% (institutional shares) in Q4, outperforming its benchmark Russell Midcap Growth Index.
  • The Russell Midcap Growth Index declined 3.70% in the quarter.
  • The fund reestablished a position in Repligen (RGEN).
  • Additionally, the fund added Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH), Birkenstock (BIRK), Welltower (WELL), and Axon Enterprise (AXON) to its portfolio during the quarter.
  • Baron Asset Fund sold Trade Desk (TTD), Gartner (IT), IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), Dayforce (DAY), and IDEX (IEX) in Q4.
  • Source: Fund Letter

data.title: Glencore nears $4B sale of Kazakhstan zinc mining business - Bloomberg
data.description:
Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) is in talks to sell its stake in Kazzinc Ltd. in Kazakhstan to a local businessman, Bloomberg reported Thursday, as part of a series of deals that could reshape the country's mining landscape.

Shakhmurat Mutalip is in discussions to purchase the miner's 70% stake in zinc and gold producer Kazzinc in a deal that could be worth $4B-$4.5B, and Glencore may extend funding for the purchase, according to the report.

Separately, Mutalip also is said to be in talks to buy 40% of Eurasian Resources Group from the families of two of the Kazakh mining group's founders and is talking to both Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) and rival trader Mercuria about funding for that transaction, the report said.

Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) has held various talks in the past year about selling its holdings across Kazakhstan, which have helped underpin its decades-long dominance in the global zinc market; Kazzinc was one of the company's most profitable assets last year, thanks largely to surging prices for gold, which it mines as a byproduct.

data.title: SiriusPoint outlines $100M share buyback and targets 16.2% ROE while expanding A&H specialty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SiriusPoint Ltd. (SPNT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott Egan highlighted a year of strong performance, stating, "Our top line for the year grew 16%. We improved the quality of our underwriting earnings year-over-year by 1.5 points. We grew our diluted book value per share by 28%. We delivered a 49% increase in operating earnings per share over prior year, and our leverage will reduce to an all-time low of 23% by the end of February." He emphasized the importance of the company’s Accident & Health (A&H) segment, noting it accounts for 27% of the business mix and grew gross written premiums by 23% to around $1 billion. Egan also announced the sale of Armada and Arcadian MGAs, which together delivered $390 million of liquidity and almost $200 million of off-balance sheet value.
  • Egan introduced two acquisitions—Assist America and World Nomads—integrated into the IMG A&H MGA, targeting a combined incremental EBITDA of $8 million to $10 million annually post-integration. He stated, "We expect our IMG business to generate over $30 million of fee income and over $35 million of EBITDA in 2026."
  • In capital management, Egan revealed, "We are pleased to be announcing our intention to repurchase $100 million of our outstanding common shares over the next 12 months. At our current market price, this represents over 4% of the total shares outstanding."
  • CFO James McKinney reported, "Net income for 2025 increased 141% or [ $216 million ] to $444 million as we delivered excellent financial results on a core and consolidated basis. Return on equity was 22.1%." He added, "Core gross written premiums grew by $134 million or 18% in the quarter versus the prior year."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its operating return on equity target of 12% to 15% across the cycle, with Egan noting, "Our 2025 operating return on equity of 16.2% has improved for the third consecutive year and more importantly, outperformed against our 12% to 15% across the cycle target."
  • Egan acknowledged that insurance market conditions will be tougher in 2026 but emphasized that "not in every market" and cited diversification and focus on specialist MGAs as positioning the company well.
  • McKinney provided expense guidance for 2026, stating, "Looking forward, for 2026, we project a similar OUE expense ratio of 6.5% to 7%."
  • Management expects IMG to generate over $30 million in fee income and over $35 million in EBITDA in 2026 following acquisitions and integration.

Financial Results

  • SiriusPoint reported operating income of $86 million or $0.70 per diluted share and net income of $240 million or $1.97 per diluted share for the fourth quarter.
  • Core gross written premiums grew by $134 million or 18% in the quarter, with A&H up 20% year-over-year. The company’s core combined ratio was 92.9% for the quarter.
  • For the full year, common shareholders' equity increased $532 million to $2.3 billion, and diluted book value per share, excluding AOCI, grew 24% to $18.10.
  • Net investment income for the quarter was $69 million, while net investment income for the year was $275 million.
  • The company’s BSCR ratio improved to 247% and is expected to be 232% pro-forma for redemption of preference shares.

Q&A

  • Michael Phillips, Oppenheimer: Asked about the sustainability of the insurance segment’s combined ratio and attritional loss ratio trends in 2026. Egan responded, "The way that we're thinking about '26 is where we don't see the opportunity to make a return commensurate with the risk... we can move capital quickly around the group and seize other opportunities." McKinney added, "I'd start exactly with that 91.7%. And I would think depending on how mix comes in over the year, where we outperform, where we see the best returns... maybe plus or minus kind of 0.5 point as a starting position."
  • Phillips asked about fee income post-Armada sale. McKinney stated, "You're in a range of about $26 million inside there. I would think about it as like a run rate of about $30 million with potential of kind of post completion of everything, I think you're looking at about a run rate base expectation of around $40 million."
  • Phillips inquired about growth in Surety and its sustainability. Egan said, "We view it as sort of pretty sustainable on a go-forward basis... we're not projecting any sort of falloff for the 2 areas that you've highlighted." McKinney added, "Minimal amounts of kind of our book follow that. I mean, we feel pretty good about where we've entered."
  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: Asked about the rate environment for casualty insurance. McKinney replied, "Relative to the specialties in the areas where we focus, we think -- and what we're seeing is that rate is broadly moving in line with trend."
  • Andersen questioned talent focus. Egan commented, "We're obviously attracting a underwriting talent to the organization, but I would also say we're attracting sort of functional talent, et cetera, as well as we sort of strengthen our capabilities."
  • Andersen asked about retention rates. McKinney responded, "We continue to see opportunity there... then you see us gradually kind of increase our net in those components."
  • Unknown Analyst, Raymond James: On the insurance vs. reinsurance mix, Egan stated, "We want to grow insurance over reinsurance. I think it fits within our sort of strategic ambition of lower volatility."
  • Regarding share buyback cadence, McKinney noted, "Likely to be a little bit kind of opportunistic, but also with we feel like we're -- we feel we're in a really attractive position from a market perspective or other."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on growth sustainability, fee income outlook, and segment mix rather than expressing major concerns.
  • Management tone was confident and forward-looking, with Egan reiterating, "We remain focused and determined to deliver against our ambitions, and we are positive about our outlook."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a consistent, upbeat tone, while analysts continued to seek clarity on growth sustainability and profitability metrics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, with ongoing emphasis on the 12% to 15% ROE target and focus on disciplined underwriting, but saw an update with the announcement of a $100 million share buyback and new acquisitions for the A&H segment.
  • Strategic focus further shifted toward growth in specialty insurance, A&H, and fee income expansion through acquisitions.
  • Key metric increases included net income, book value per share, and core premium growth rates, all higher than those stated in the previous quarter.
  • Management’s tone continued to be positive, with repeated emphasis on performance momentum, while analysts’ tone remained constructive and focused on operational details.
  • Evolution in management confidence was evident, especially in capital allocation and business mix strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged tougher insurance market conditions for 2026 but stressed the company’s diversification and agility in capital deployment.
  • Catastrophe losses, particularly from California wildfires, impacted results, but the company cited risk transfer actions and new aggregate reinsurance coverage to manage volatility.
  • Management indicated a prudent approach to increasing retention within MGA partnerships and maintaining a disciplined underwriting approach in the face of changing market conditions.

Final Takeaway

SiriusPoint closed 2025 with robust growth in premiums, significant earnings and book value expansion, and a disciplined approach to both underwriting and capital management. The company’s focus on specialty lines, particularly Accident & Health, and its ongoing MGA rationalization and acquisitions underpin management’s confidence in sustainable, profitable growth. The newly announced $100 million share buyback and low leverage position further support capital flexibility, while the outlook for 2026 remains positive despite anticipated market challenges, backed by strong underwriting discipline and portfolio diversification.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Claros outlines $2.5B loan resolutions and signals portfolio expansion after 2026 as balance sheet strengthens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Claros Mortgage Trust, Inc. (CMTG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Richard Mack stated that "CMTG made a meaningful amount of progress last year, executing on several critical path items," including resolving watchlist loans, enhancing liquidity, and deleveraging the portfolio. He highlighted exceeding the $2 billion total resolution target by closing the year with $2.5 billion in total resolutions, including the resolution of 11 watchlist loans with an aggregate UPB of $1.3 billion. The company generated significant liquidity throughout the year, which was used to delever the portfolio and reduce corporate debt. Mack added, "We retired the Term Loan B that was scheduled to mature in August of 2026," replacing it with a new $500 million senior secured loan from HPS, extending the maturity of corporate debt to 2030.
  • Mack explained that for 2026, the focus remains on asset management and execution on resolving watchlist loans and REO assets, with the goal to "begin to evaluate new lending opportunities towards the end of 2026 and lay the groundwork for portfolio growth in subsequent years."
  • President and CFO John McGillis reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, CMTG reported a GAAP net loss of $1.56 per share and a distributable loss of $0.71 per share. Distributable earnings prior to realized gains and losses were $0.02 per share." McGillis noted the held-for-investment loan portfolio declined to $3.7 billion at year-end, down from $4.3 billion at September 30 and $6.1 billion at year-end 2024, as the company reduced exposure to sectors facing headwinds and prepared for a return to originations.
  • McGillis detailed that the quarter included the resolution of four loans, generating liquidity and reducing office and land exposure, and a $46 million principal charge-off on a Connecticut office loan. He also highlighted resolving an $88 million New York City land loan through foreclosure and discussed portfolio credit events, including downgrades of several loans and a provision for current expected credit losses of $212 million.

Outlook

  • Mack indicated that 2026 will be focused on resolving watchlist loans and working through REO assets, aiming to position the company for new lending opportunities towards the end of the year. He stated, "Our goal is to position the company to begin to evaluate new lending opportunities towards the end of 2026 and lay the groundwork for portfolio growth in subsequent years."
  • Management expressed optimism based on encouraging property market fundamentals, including "a reduction in new supply, tightening credit spreads and improving financing costs for new originations."

Financial Results

  • McGillis reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, CMTG reported a GAAP net loss of $1.56 per share and a distributable loss of $0.71 per share. Distributable earnings prior to realized gains and losses were $0.02 per share." The held-for-investment loan portfolio declined to $3.7 billion at December 31, compared to $4.3 billion at September 30.
  • The company’s total CECL reserve on loans receivable held for investment increased to $443 million or 10.9% of UPB at year-end, up from $308 million or 6.8% at September 30. The general CECL reserve decreased to $78 million or 2.9% of UPB.
  • Liquidity rose to $153 million following the new senior secured term loan, representing a $51 million increase compared to the prior year-end. Net debt-to-equity ratio decreased from 2.4x at December 31, 2024, to 1.9x at December 31, 2025.

Q&A

  • Richard Shane, JPMorgan: Asked about extracting or creating shareholder value outside portfolio resolutions, given the stock’s discount to book. Mack replied, "Our goal right now has to be cleaning the book up so that it is a much more transparent and easier to understand business."
  • Shane questioned the likely trend in net interest income (NII) in Q1 and Q2 2026. McGillis answered, "That top line interest income level is going to continue to compress...that net interest income line is going to be choppy until we sort of turn the corner on the book and can get back to originations."
  • Shane asked about timing for translating reserves into losses. Priyanka Garg responded, "We think we have a good sense of the reserves that we need to take in order to accelerate resolutions and turn over the book...there can be new information and we might have changes in this really dynamic environment."
  • John Nickodemus, BTIG: Asked about liquidity trajectory in 2026. McGillis stated, "We now have a pretty significant level of liquidity cushion over a minimum liquidity requirement...success for this year, by the end of the year, we're evaluating a variety of capital allocation options for available liquidity."
  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP: Inquired about NOI in the REO portfolio and CapEx expectations. Garg explained, "In the multifamily assets that we have, REO, it is a mixed bag. There are some that are generating real NOI, others that are a little bit more challenged...it's all part of the plan and certainly was expected."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about the long path to value creation and ongoing portfolio challenges, with a slightly negative to neutral tone, especially regarding NII and the pace of resolutions.
  • Management maintained an optimistic and measured tone, emphasizing progress, but acknowledged challenges. Mack stated, "We are really excited to be feeling like we're -- we can see the light at the end of the tunnel and that the capital markets are cooperating with us."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism remains, while management’s confidence level has increased due to recent achievements and the new financing agreement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to a more detailed discussion of portfolio cleanup, with explicit mention of returning to new originations after 2026.
  • There was a significant increase in loan resolutions ($2.5 billion vs. $2.3 billion reported last quarter), continued focus on liquidity, and a new debt facility extending maturities to 2030.
  • Analysts maintained focus on NII trends, liquidity, and timing of asset resolutions, similar to the previous quarter’s questions.
  • Management’s tone is more upbeat, signaling progress on key strategic priorities and a pathway toward portfolio expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing portfolio risk with multiple loan downgrades and substantial CECL provisions, indicating continued asset quality challenges.
  • The company faces uncertainty in the commercial real estate market, especially regarding the timing and magnitude of recovery.
  • Analysts questioned the pace of resolutions and the impact on earnings, particularly as net interest income is expected to remain volatile.

Final Takeaway

Claros Mortgage Trust completed $2.5 billion in loan resolutions in 2025, strengthened its balance sheet through deleveraging, and replaced a near-term maturing term loan with a new facility maturing in 2030. Management remains focused on resolving watchlist loans and REO assets in 2026, with the expectation of evaluating new lending opportunities toward the end of the year and setting the stage for future portfolio growth. The company believes its current liquidity position and capital structure improvements provide flexibility to continue executing its business plan and support shareholder value as market conditions improve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ClearBridge International Value Strategy adds RHHBY and MKGAF; exits GRRMF, AKESF, and HKMPF
data.description:
• ClearBridge International Value Strategy outperformed its benchmark in Q4 2025, as the MSCI EAFE Index rose 4.9% during the quarter, compared with a 2.7% increase in the S&P 500.
  • During Q4 2025, the strategy initiated new positions in Roche (RHHBY) and Merck KGaA (MKGAF).
  • In Q4 2025, the strategy exited Gerresheimer (GRRMF), Akeso (AKESF), and Hikma Pharmaceuticals (HKMPF).
  • Source.

data.title: Technology weight is becoming a headwind for the S&P 500 – Goldman Sachs’s Ben Snider
data.description:
The heavy concentration of technology stocks (VGT), (XLK), (IYW) in the S&P 500 (SP500) has transformed from a major tailwind into a drag on the index’s performance, according to Ben Snider, Goldman Sachs chief U.S. equity strategist.

Despite solid economic data and strong fourth-quarter earnings, the benchmark index (SP500) remains roughly flat year-to-date. “In aggregate, that tech weight is becoming a little bit of a headwind,” Snider told CNBC.

The divergence between the cap-weighted index and the broader market is striking. The equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) is up approximately 5%-6% this year, with about 350 of 500 stocks trading higher. Snider characterized the underlying market as “pretty good,” even as the headline index struggles to gain ground.

The shift in the artificial intelligence narrative has been a key factor weighing on technology stocks. According to Snider, the AI story has evolved from “purely optimism to now some balance of optimism and disruption.”

The software industry (IGV), (XSW), (IGPT) has experienced a roughly 30% downturn, though he noted valuations remain elevated at around 20x earnings, and that the market is now pricing in an “average trajectory of growth, which, given the tremendous amount of uncertainty going forward, seems like a reasonable place to be.”

Despite the challenges facing technology shares, Snider remains optimistic about corporate profitability. He predicted that S&P 500 (SP500) profit margins will reach new record highs.

“By the end of this year, we’ll be at a new record,” he said.

He attributed this outlook to operating leverage in a healthy revenue growth environment and the outsized earnings power of large technology companies.

The primary risk to equities, according to Snider, would be any data that causes investors to doubt the growth outlook. While he acknowledged that much of tech has experienced significant volatility, the broader index has held up remarkably well due to signs of economic acceleration.

“So far so good,” he said, “but we’re keeping a close eye.”

data.title: Repsol says Venezuela owes $5.4B but recovery not a top priority for now
data.description:
Repsol (REPYY) (REPYF) is owed €4.55B (~$5.37B) by Venezuela, including debt for oil and gas supplies, arrears in payment of interests, and financing for its Petroquiriquire joint venture with state oil firm PDVSA, the Spanish company said in its annual report Thursday.

Repsol (REPYY) (REPYF) has been active in Venezuela for more than 30 years and is one of the companies allowed to run oil and gas operations in the country under a general license recently issued by the U.S. Treasury Department.

While recouping the outstanding debt has long been an important issue, CEO Josu Jon Imaz said it is not a top priority for now.

"It's time to recover a normal commercial operation, which means being paid for the production we have," and Repsol (REPYY) (REPYF) is working to increase the amount of gas it produces and supplies in Venezuela by 10% to ~640M cf/day, Imaz said on the company's earnings conference call, according to Reuters.

The company also can increase oil output by 50% in 12 months and triple it within three years, the CEO said; its production in Venezuela last year averaged 71.3K boe/day.

"If Venezuela recovers normal production levels, if the economic development of the country evolves in the right way, I'm sure we're going to find frameworks and solutions to talk about the past," Imaz said, referring to the debt.

The comments came as Repsol (REPYY) (REPYF) reported Q4 adjusted earnings rose to €661M from €587M in the year-earlier quarter and swung to a net profit of €722M from a €36M loss a year ago, when it booked significant charges.

The company guided for FY 2026 total production of 560K-570K boe/day.

data.title: Quanta Services targets over 20% adjusted EPS growth in 2026 amid record $44B backlog and vertical integration initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quanta Services (PWR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Earl Austin, President and CEO, opened the call by highlighting Quanta's continued record-breaking performance, stating, "2025 was another year of significant achievement and advancement for Quanta. Again, we delivered record results as we generated double-digit growth in revenues, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted earnings per share, along with record free cash flow and backlog." He emphasized the company's position as a "compounder of profitable growth" and the success of strategic acquisitions, with 8 completed in 2025, expanding both capabilities and workforce to approximately 69,500 employees.

  • Austin noted momentum across end markets, citing a $44 billion backlog. He called out the October selection by NiSource for a 3-gigawatt power project in Indiana as a key example of Quanta's solutions platform. The company is investing $500 million to $700 million over several years in power transformer manufacturing facilities and vertical supply chain strategy, aiming to differentiate in the high-voltage transmission market.

  • Austin stated, “We believe Quanta is uniquely positioned at the center of a multi-decade infrastructure transformation, and we are confident in our ability to generate attractive compounding returns and deliver long-term stakeholder value.”

  • CFO Jayshree Desai said, "We are pleased to report another quarter of strong execution, capping a year in which Quanta delivered record results across virtually every key financial metric." Desai reported full year revenues of $28.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $2.9 billion, and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $10.75. She highlighted record cash flow from operations of $2.2 billion and free cash flow of $1.7 billion for 2025, with Q4 revenues at $7.8 billion and adjusted diluted EPS at $3.16. She also noted, “During the fourth quarter, we completed 3 acquisitions... for aggregate upfront consideration of approximately $1.7 billion... Even after deploying this level of capital... we maintained a leverage ratio below 2x.”

  • Desai outlined 2026 expectations with continued double-digit growth in revenues, net income and adjusted EBITDA, and the opportunity to deliver over 20% growth in adjusted EPS, supported by record backlog and broad-based market strength.

Outlook

  • Management expects continued double-digit growth in revenues, net income, and adjusted EBITDA for 2026, and targets over 20% growth in adjusted EPS. Desai explained that these expectations are "supported by record backlog at year-end of $44 billion, the strength of which is broad-based, driven by ongoing investment in grid reliability and resilience, growing demand for power generation and the long-term infrastructure investment required to meet rising electricity consumption across the economy."

  • The company anticipates free cash flow of $1.8 billion at the midpoint of its range, including $250 million to $350 million of expected capital expenditures related to vertical supply chain initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Quanta reported full year revenues of $28.5 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $2.9 billion, and adjusted diluted earnings per share of $10.75. Record cash flow from operations reached $2.2 billion for the year, with free cash flow at $1.7 billion.

  • Fourth quarter revenues were $7.8 billion, with adjusted EBITDA of $845 million and adjusted diluted EPS of $3.16. Cash flow from operations in the quarter was $1.1 billion, and free cash flow was $946 million.

  • Three acquisitions in Q4 cost $1.7 billion in aggregate, funded by cash and common stock, and the company maintained leverage below 2x after these investments.

Q&A

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies LLC: Asked about the outlook for high teens earnings growth and data center contracts in 2026. Austin responded that data centers are "roughly like 10% of the business at this point," with backlog growing and significant opportunity ahead, suggesting a multi-decade growth trajectory.

  • Steven Fisher, UBS: Inquired about electric margin dynamics and vertical integration. Austin noted solid broad-based growth across markets for 2026, and clarified there are no "major large projects" starting in 2026, with margin initiatives tied to vertical supply chain investments.

  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities: Asked about additional CCGT projects and margin targets. Austin indicated ongoing joint ventures and prudent margin guidance, emphasizing risk management and programmatic, long-term collaboration with customers.

  • Manish Somaiya, Cantor Fitzgerald: Questioned supply chain and pricing discipline. Austin discussed vertical supply chain investments to "derisk the supply chain" and highlighted a shift to longer-term, negotiated programmatic spend rather than short-term bidding.

  • Mark Strouse, JPMorgan: Asked about the gas power generation pipeline. Austin stated backlog growth is expected, with risk-adjusted booking for generation projects both in JV settings and internally, pointing to a build ramp in '27-'29.

  • Additional questions covered programmatic customer relationships, labor tightness (notably in data center-related roles), renewables pipeline, large transmission project visibility, custom fabrication capabilities, M&A opportunities, and AI initiatives for operational efficiency.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions centered on growth durability, margin management, labor constraints, and supply chain strategy, with a generally positive and forward-looking tone.

  • Management maintained a confident and strategic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing backlog growth, risk management, and multi-decade opportunities. Austin used phrases such as “We are confident in Quanta's ability…” and “I like our chances to continue what we've done in the past.”

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains optimistic but with increased emphasis on vertical integration, labor market tightness, and strategic capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • 2025 Q4 saw record results across revenues, EBITDA, EPS, and cash flow, a step up from Q3’s already strong performance and record backlog of $39.2 billion (now $44 billion).

  • Guidance language shifted from projecting double-digit EPS growth for 2026 to specifically stating an opportunity for over 20% adjusted EPS growth.

  • Strategic focus has evolved from announcing the total solutions platform and NiSource project in Q3 to emphasizing execution on vertical supply chain investments, multiple acquisitions, and expanded self-perform capabilities in Q4.

  • Analyst questions shifted from basic platform expansion and risk management (Q3) to details on data center exposure, labor pressures, and supply chain mitigation (Q4).

  • Management’s confidence remained high, with increased discussion of vertical supply chain investments and industry positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified labor market tightness, particularly in data center projects, as a challenge, with ongoing investment in talent pipelines and training.

  • Supply chain risks are being mitigated through $500 million to $700 million investments in power transformer manufacturing and vertical integration.

  • Margin pressures from regulated utility work and workforce expansion were acknowledged, but risk-adjusted project booking and long-term customer relationships are core mitigation strategies.

  • Analysts raised questions about market pricing discipline, renewables policy uncertainties, and the scalability of supply chain and labor initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Quanta Services closed 2025 with record financials and a $44 billion backlog, signaling strong momentum for 2026. Management projects continued double-digit growth, targeting over 20% adjusted EPS growth, supported by strategic acquisitions, vertical integration, and expanding solutions for data centers, utilities, and power generation. While labor tightness and supply chain risks remain, the company’s disciplined approach and robust backlog position it for sustained compounding returns and long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DoorDash faces questions over investment intensity and impact on margins
data.description:
DoorDash (DASH) is in the green for a third consecutive day but off its best levels during Thursday’s trading session as investors continue to digest the company’s fourth quarter performance and the impact its heavy investments in DashMart, DashLink, and Deliveroo will have on profitability going forward.

The food delivery company enjoyed better than expected gains in total orders and gross order value ((GOV)), and total sales and per share earnings both increased by double digits from the same quarter last year (although missed expectations).

But the company’s margins were weighed down by investments with gross margin, net revenue margin, and adjusted EBITDA as a percentage of marketplace GOV, all lower from a year earlier.  This will likely continue to be an issue for investors given DoorDash’s (DASH) assurances that it anticipates “significant levels of ongoing investment in new categories and international markets.”

Wedbush’s Scott Devitt, however, thinks this high level of investment is justified as they “expand DoorDash’s addressable market on a global scale and support more durable long-term growth.”

This was reflected in DoorDash’s (DASH) year-over-year growth in the fourth quarter in grocery and retail and higher engagement from new cohorts.

“Importantly, DoorDash now expects unit economics in the U.S. grocery and retail categories will turn positive in the second half of 2026,” Devitt adds.

UBS analyst Stephen Ju, however, thinks it’s unlikely DoorDash (DASH) can deliver a beat on adjusted EBITDA given the ongoing investment level, leading Ju to maintain his Neutral rating on the stock.

“The investment intensity remains unanswered,” Ju says, adding that questions about 2027 cost centers remain unresolved.

“This does result in a decreased conviction level in our estimates, especially in a market in which investors are asking for clearer answers on ROIC. As such, our narrow risk/reward skew of 1.7x leaves us Neutral rated,” Ju says.

Wedbush’s Devitt retains his Outperform rating, however, as the company’s “shared guidance commentary this quarter likely cleared investor concerns by offering greater visibility into the company’s required level of investment in 2026."

“One of the major areas of spend is our global tech stack,” CFO Ravi Inukonda said on the company’s earnings call.

“We believe these investments are the right investments, especially as we think about becoming the operating system for local commerce. As we make more progress, we’ll continue to invest behind them because ultimately, they lead to more areas in which we can invest and improve overall free cash flow,” Inukonda said.

data.title: Enlight Renewable Energy secures NIS 1.32B in private placement
data.description:
• Enlight Renewable Energy (ENLT) received commitments from Israeli institutional investors to purchase 6M ordinary shares at NIS 220 per share.
  • The private placement will generate aggregate gross proceeds of ~NIS 1.32B.
  • Net proceeds will support ENLT's strategic growth plans across geographies and strengthen its balance sheet.
  • ENLT shares down 5%.
